title: The New Carbon Order: China’s Response to Europe’s CBAM
gist: CSIS argues the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) is acting as a forcing function that quietly accelerates China's domestic carbon governance—ETS expansion, plant-level MRV, and a rapid buildout of local carbon-accounting bureaus—pushing China's institutions toward EU convergence. Near-term CBAM cost to China is tiny (~$1.4bn, 0.3% of EU exports), but 2028 scope expansion to ~180 downstream steel/aluminum products plus a planned shift to absolute caps and paid permits by 2030 materially raises the stakes, with the US risking exclusion from an emerging EU-China carbon-adjusted trading bloc.

insights.claim: EU CBAM certificates were priced at €75.36 per ton of CO2 in Q1 2026, and from 2027 certificate prices will be set by the weekly average auction price of EU ETS allowances, directly linking import carbon costs to the EU's domestic carbon market.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Carbon import costs for steel/aluminum/cement exporters now track a volatile market price; watch EU ETS allowance auctions as the leading indicator of CBAM cost escalation.
insights.quote: For the first quarter of 2026, CBAM certificates were priced at €75.36 per ton of CO2 emitted. Starting in 2027, certificate prices will be calculated using the weekly average auction price of EU ETS allowances.

insights.claim: CBAM functions as an extraterritorial incentive: the tariff can only be avoided by proving an equivalent carbon price was paid in the country of production, pushing other jurisdictions to build EU-compatible carbon pricing and governance.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: CBAM is a regulatory export mechanism, not just a tariff—expect partner countries to stand up carbon-pricing regimes, creating demand for MRV, certification, and carbon-data services.
insights.quote: As this tariff can only be avoided by proving that an equivalent carbon price has been paid elsewhere, CBAM creates an incentive for other jurisdictions to develop credible carbon pricing and governance systems compatible with strict EU standards.

insights.claim: CBAM's near-term cost to China is small—covered categories made up only 0.8% of China's total EU exports in 2025, and one 2025 study put the cost at $1.4 billion, roughly 0.3% of China's EU export value.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Current CBAM is not a material China macro risk; the trade signal is in future scope expansion, not today's exposure.
insights.quote: Goods from the six covered categories made up only 0.8 percent of China’s total EU exports in 2025, according to customs data. Iron and steel accounted for 92 percent of affected exports, followed by aluminum at 7 percent.

insights.claim: China is the EU's single largest exporter at 21.3% of 2024 EU imports (vs 13.8% for the US), and its share of CBAM-covered exports is the second highest of any partner, trailing only Russia.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: China's scale makes it the structurally most exposed partner as CBAM widens—position for rising carbon-cost pass-through in China-origin EU-bound goods over time.
insights.quote: In 2024, 21.3 percent of goods imported into the European Union originated in China. This makes China the single largest exporter to the bloc, dwarfing the 13.8 percent from the United States, its second-largest trade partner.

insights.claim: CBAM is expected to expand in 2028 to roughly 180 steel- and aluminum-intensive downstream products including machinery components and household appliances, sharply increasing the carbon-cost burden on Chinese exporters along the value chain.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: The 2028 downstream expansion is the real trade catalyst—appliance and machinery exporters with high embedded steel/aluminum become newly exposed; monitor the covered-product list.
insights.quote: Starting in 2028, CBAM is expected to expand to about 180 steel- and aluminum-intensive products, including machinery components and household appliances.

insights.claim: A 2021 State Council Development Research Center report estimated that in an extreme scenario where CBAM covered all Chinese exports, China's GDP could fall up to 0.64%, putting 2.3 million manufacturing jobs at risk and cutting EU exports ~13% by 2030.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Quantifies Beijing's internal tail-risk view of carbon tariffs—useful for gauging how seriously China treats CBAM even if full coverage is unlikely.
insights.quote: China’s GDP could fall by up to 0.64 percent, putting 2.3 million manufacturing jobs at risk and leading exports to the European Union to decline by around 13 percent by 2030.

themes.theme: China energy system and carbon intensity

insights.claim: China remains structurally carbon-intensive—in 2024 it emitted 0.39 kg CO2e per PPP dollar of GDP, roughly four times the EU average and twice the global average, keeping its export goods carbon-heavy regardless of headline renewable growth.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: China's per-output carbon intensity is the root of CBAM exposure; renewables growth alone won't close the gap fast enough to neutralize product-level footprints.
insights.quote: In 2024, China emitted 0.39 kilograms of CO2e (carbon dioxide equivalent) per U.S. dollar of GDP on a purchasing power parity basis—roughly four times the EU average and twice the global average.

insights.claim: Coal still dominates China's system—53% of total energy consumption and 58% of power generation in 2024, and ~34% of industrial energy inputs in 2023 (vs the EU's 9%), entrenching high embedded emissions in industrial exports.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Industrial coal intensity is the binding constraint on lowering Chinese export carbon footprints; coal's role in grid flexibility means decarbonization is slower than capacity-addition headlines imply.
insights.quote: As of 2024, coal accounted for 53 percent of China’s total energy consumption and 58 percent of its power generation, while non-fossil sources supplied roughly one-fifth of primary energy and one-third of electricity.

insights.claim: Data centers could emerge as a new pressure point on China's power demand and emissions, alongside a manufacturing-focused Covid recovery strategy that likely caused China to miss its 2025 Paris carbon-intensity targets.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: AI/data-center load growth is a forward driver of Chinese coal demand and emissions—watch as a structural headwind to China's intensity targets and a potential CBAM cost amplifier.
insights.quote: Though not yet a major source of electricity demand growth, data centers could also emerge as a new pressure point alongside China’s manufacturing-focused Covid recovery strategy, which has likely already contributed to the country missing its 2025 carbon intensity targets under the Paris Agreement.

themes.theme: China ETS and carbon governance buildout

insights.claim: China's national ETS now covers power, steel, cement, and aluminum—~3,000 firms and ~60% of national emissions—but remains light-touch with mostly free allowances and intensity-based (not absolute-cap) compliance.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: China's carbon price signal is still weak; the investable shift is the planned move to absolute caps and paid permits, which would create a real domestic carbon cost.
insights.quote: Today, China’s national ETS encompasses the power sector as well as steel, cement, and aluminum—carbon-intensive industries covered by CBAM—spanning roughly 3,000 firms and about 60 percent of national emissions.

insights.claim: China's ETS is expected to expand to chemicals, petrochemicals, paper, aviation, flat glass, and copper smelting, with a planned shift toward absolute caps and paid permits by 2030, bringing its market closer to the EU ETS.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Sector expansion plus paid permits by 2030 would lift carbon costs for a far broader set of Chinese industrials—track the cap-and-permit transition as the key inflection.
insights.quote: China’s ETS is expected to expand to cover additional high-emission sectors, including chemicals, petrochemicals, paper, aviation, flat glass, and copper smelting, alongside a planned shift toward absolute caps and paid permits by 2030.

insights.claim: CBAM is forcing Chinese firms to upgrade from annual-average emissions reporting to high-frequency, plant-specific MRV (monitoring, reporting, verification), even though near-term carbon-price burden stays low.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: The binding compliance cost is data infrastructure, not the carbon price itself—creates demand for carbon-accounting, MRV software, and certification providers.
insights.quote: they will increasingly need to strengthen monitoring, reporting, and verification systems to disclose high-frequency, plant-specific data, rather than relying on annual averages.

insights.claim: China publicly attacks CBAM as protectionist (most recently Jan 2026 by the Ministry of Commerce) yet, unlike Russia, has not filed a formal WTO case, instead quietly letting local authorities and firms work out compliance.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: China's behavior contradicts its rhetoric—Beijing is effectively accommodating CBAM, signaling it will adapt rather than retaliate; discount the protest language.
insights.quote: In practice, China’s policy responses have been considerably more pragmatic than its rhetoric suggests.
insights.locator: Rhetorical Resistance and Pragmatic Response to CBAM

insights.claim: Chinese experts and officials view CBAM's incentives as aligned with China's own 30/60 (peak before 2030, neutrality before 2060) goals, with some crediting CBAM directly for the expansion of China's ETS.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: CBAM acts as a forcing mechanism that accelerates pre-existing Chinese policy—external regulation is reshaping domestic reform pace, a template the US may later face or deploy.
insights.quote: The expansion of the Chinese ETS system, credited to CBAM by some experts interviewed by the authors, mirrors those goals.
insights.locator: Rhetorical Resistance and Pragmatic Response to CBAM

insights.claim: China has officially announced 30 provincial-level carbon-accounting platforms, but only seven were confirmed operational as of April 2026 (11 planning, 11 pilot), with all active bureaus established after 2024—evidence of a rapid but immature institutional buildout spurred by CBAM.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable buildout of Chinese carbon-data infrastructure—the 2027 wave of platform launches is a near-term milestone signaling MRV maturation and CBAM readiness.
insights.quote: According to the authors’ calculation, 30 provincial-level carbon accounting platforms have been officially announced... with only seven platforms confirmed to be in operation as of April 2026.
insights.locator: Deep Dive: The Role of Local Governments

insights.claim: The wealthiest, export-heavy provinces—Guangdong (Shenzhen), Zhejiang, Jiangsu, Shanghai—are leading carbon-accounting adoption, meaning implementation capacity will be highly uneven across China.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Carbon-compliance capacity clusters in rich coastal export hubs—firms there will be CBAM-ready first, a differentiator in export competitiveness for lower-carbon goods.
insights.quote: Many of the earliest localities to establish carbon accounting bureaus, such as Guangdong, Zhejiang, Jiangsu, and Shanghai, are among the wealthiest in China and home to many exporting enterprises.
insights.locator: Deep Dive: The Role of Local Governments

insights.claim: Bureaucratic infighting is slowing China's carbon governance—the powerful NDRC versus a relatively weak Ministry of Ecology and Environment, plus NEA-MEE conflict, has delayed carbon-accounting standards and the ETS national plan.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Implementation risk is institutional, not technical—delays in standard-setting could push back CBAM-equivalence credibility and the timeline for EU-China carbon alignment.
insights.quote: More recently, conflict between the National Energy Agency and the MEE has also been identified as delaying the publication of carbon accounting standards.
insights.locator: Deep Dive: The Role of Local Governments

insights.claim: China produces ~60% of global aluminum (~5% of China's total emissions) and since 2017 has relocated as much as 30% of domestic aluminum capacity to clean-energy regions, with 2024 province-specific renewable targets ranging from 21% (Shandong) to 70% (Yunnan).
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Aluminum is the leading edge of Chinese export decarbonization—relocation to hydro-rich Yunnan plus green-aluminum certification could give Chinese producers a low-carbon export edge under CBAM.
insights.quote: since 2017, Chinese firms have relocated as much as 30 percent of domestic aluminum production capacity to regions with abundant clean energy sources.
insights.locator: Intersections with Broader Decarbonization Efforts: A Case Study in Aluminum

insights.claim: China set 2025 targets to boost recycled aluminum, which uses only ~5% of the energy of conventional production, and is promoting 'zero-carbon' industrial parks to replace ~15,000 existing parks—both aimed at lowering export carbon footprints.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Recycled aluminum and zero-carbon parks are explicit levers to cut embedded emissions for tradable commodities—watch green-aluminum certification and trading as an emerging premium market.
insights.quote: targets were set in 2025 to boost recycled aluminum production, which requires only about 5 percent of the energy used in conventional processes.
insights.locator: Intersections with Broader Decarbonization Efforts: A Case Study in Aluminum

themes.theme: Strategic implications for US and trade blocs

insights.claim: Deeper EU-China carbon institutional alignment could leave the US outside a large carbon-adjusted trading bloc and complicate transatlantic coordination, with the rules being 'rewritten in its absence' during US retrenchment.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Carbon governance is becoming a trade-bloc dividing line—US absence is a strategic risk; carbon-tariff policy is now a US-China economic competition lever to watch.
insights.quote: When the United States chooses to reengage with global emissions governance, it may find that the rules have been rewritten in its absence.

insights.claim: China may narrow its carbon-intensity gap with the US through sustained intervention, especially if the US fails to make the investments needed to preserve its carbon-intensity advantage.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Carbon intensity is reframed as a competitiveness asset—US policy inaction could erode a structural advantage that matters for future carbon-adjusted trade access.
insights.quote: China’s policy evolution in recent years suggests that it may be able to narrow its carbon gap with the United States through coordinated and sustained interventions, especially if the latter fails to make the investments and policy commitments needed to preserve its carbon-intensity advantage.

insights.claim: The EU's Industrial Accelerator Act (IAA), which adds local content requirements, signals a possible shift toward protectionism that could change China's incentives—Beijing has already criticized the IAA as protectionist.
insights.speaker: Ilaria Mazzocco and Ray Cai
insights.so_what: If CBAM/IAA tilt from decarbonization toward protectionism, China's incentive to converge on EU carbon institutions weakens—a key risk to the whole alignment thesis.
insights.quote: If CBAM becomes more protectionist than decarbonization-oriented, incentives for countries including China would change accordingly.
